title: Visualizing What Filters Learn
concept: When researchers visualize what makes each filter fire most strongly, a hierarchy appears: early-layer filters respond to simple edges, colors, and textures, while deeper-layer filters respond to complex shapes, object parts, and even whole objects.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does layer 3 of a CNN actually take as input?
exercises.explain: Right — each layer only ever sees the previous layer's feature maps. Now: what do those deep layers actually end up detecting?

cards.title: Peeking inside a trained network
cards.body: You've learned that filters detect patterns and stack into layers — but what patterns do trained filters actually end up looking for? Researchers found a way to find each filter's 'favorite' pattern, and the results form a surprisingly clean hierarchy.

cards.title: Simple to complex, layer by layer
cards.body: When you visualize what makes each filter fire strongest, early layers turn out to detect simple things — edges, color blobs, and basic textures. Deeper layers detect increasingly complex things — shapes, object parts (like an eye or a wheel), and sometimes even whole recognizable objects.

cards.title: Letters, then words, then sentences
cards.body: Learning to read follows the same buildup. First you learn individual letters (edges). Then you combine letters into words (shapes and textures). Then words become sentences (object parts). Then sentences become a whole story (a recognized object). No stage skips ahead — each one is built entirely from the stage before it.

cards.title: From a diagonal line to a dog's face
cards.body: In a trained image-recognition network, layer 1 filters often turn out to look like tiny diagonal lines, color blobs, or edge detectors — almost identical to the classical filters you already learned. By the middle layers, filters fire strongest for textures like fur or checkered patterns. By the deep layers, some filters fire strongest specifically for dog faces, car wheels, or human eyes, no matter where in the photo they appear.

cards.title: Not every deep filter has a tidy name
cards.body: It's tempting to think every deep filter cleanly represents one human concept, like 'dog face' or 'stop sign.' Many do look that clean in examples — but plenty of real filters respond to blurry mixtures of related things, not one clean labeled idea. The simple flashcard version is a useful summary, not a perfect rule.

cards.title: Early Layer vs. Deep Layer
cards.body: What each depth's filters tend to detect:

exercises.prompt: What do filters in the EARLIEST layers of a trained CNN typically end up detecting?

options.text: Simple things like edges, colors, and basic textures
Whole objects like dogs or cars
Nothing — early filters stay random forever

exercises.explain: Right — early layers learn the simplest, most general building blocks.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Filters in deeper CNN layers tend to detect more complex things, like object parts, than filters in early layers.
exercises.explain: True — complexity builds up with depth, since each layer combines what came before it.

exercises.sentence: Visualized filters reveal a hierarchy: simple patterns near the input, and increasingly ___ patterns the deeper you go.
exercises.explain: Complex — that hierarchy from simple to complex is the core insight of this lesson.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best matches what researchers actually found when visualizing trained CNN filters?

options.text: Early layers look like edge/color detectors; deep layers look like detectors for shapes and object parts
Every layer, from first to last, detects the exact same kind of simple edges
Deep layers randomly detect unrelated noise with no pattern at all

exercises.explain: Exactly — that early-to-complex hierarchy is a real, well-documented finding.

exercises.prompt: You visualize a CNN and find that a layer-2 filter fires strongest for diagonal stripes, while a layer-8 filter fires strongest specifically for human eyes. What does this pair of results demonstrate?

options.text: The exact hierarchy this lesson covers: shallow layers detect simple patterns, deep layers detect complex, specific ones built from those simple patterns
That the network made an error, since both layers should detect the same kind of pattern
That layer 8 sees the raw image just as directly as layer 2 does
That filters are assigned their job randomly and layer depth doesn't matter

exercises.explain: Exactly right! That's the layer-by-layer hierarchy in action — simple near the input, complex and specific deep inside. Next: what happens to a feature map's size and count as you go deeper.
